generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same place you’ve been for five long years—far from me, far from home, and far from the truth. I’ve written this with trembling hands, by the dim glow of the lighthouse lamp, as the wind howls like a vengeful spirit through the cliffs. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may take more than just the light from our tower.

You were not the first to leave me, nor will you be the last. But you were the first to take your silence with you, and the last to speak your mind. I have heard the tales, of course. The rumors that you were taken by the sea, or that you ran off with a sailor, or that you turned your back on the family name. I’ve heard them all, and I’ve chosen not to believe a single word.

I have kept the lighthouse these past five years, as I always have. The light still burns, though I’ve grown old and weary. The sea has been kind to me, though not always gentle. I’ve watched the tides rise and fall, the moon wax and wane, and the stars blink in the sky like distant memories. I’ve watched the world change, and I’ve watched you change, too. I’ve watched you walk away, and I’ve watched you forget.

I have never written

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I've been meaning to write for years, but the sea has always been more patient than I. Tonight, however, the sea is restless, and I feel it in my bones. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see another dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of watching the waves roll in and out, of hearing the wind howl like a beast in mourning. I have not spoken to you, not once, and I do not know if I ever will. But tonight, as I stand upon the cliffs, the wind howling like a banshee, I feel the need to speak, to tell you the truth of what I have carried all these years.

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light in my soul. I watched you grow, I watched you become a woman, and I watched you choose a life that was not mine. You chose the city, the ballrooms, the society of the well-dressed and the well-spoken. I chose the sea, the salt, the silence. And in choosing, we parted.

I have no regrets, not really. I have lived with the sea, and it has given me more than I could ever have asked for. It has given me purpose, a sense of duty,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better spirits than I find myself. It has been five years since we last spoke, and I have spent them in silence, save for the howl of the sea and the ticking of the clock in this old lighthouse. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of a man who has spent his life watching the horizon, and now sees it with a fear he has never known before.

Tonight, the storm comes. The sky has been dark for days, the wind rising like a beast in the night. The sea, once a gentle companion, now roars with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have seen many storms in my years here, but none like this. The waves crash against the cliffs with a force that shakes the very foundations of this tower. I do not know if I will see dawn. And if I do, I do not know if I will see you again.

I have not written to you in five years, and I know I have no right to ask for your forgiveness. I have been a fool, Eleanor, and a coward. I let the sea take more than it gave me, and I let my pride keep me from the one person who ever truly understood me. You were my light, my anchor in a world that was always shifting. And I let it slip away.

I remember the day you left

**April 12th, 1893**  
**Northumberland Lighthouse, Shetland Isles**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea is restless, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and I pray it will guide those who sail these waters. But I fear the storm may be too great, and I may not live to see dawn.

You have not written to me in five years, Eliza. I have not written to you either. We have grown distant, like the lighthouse and the shore—close, yet separated by an unbridgeable chasm. I have tried to forget, but the weight of silence is heavy upon my heart. I have thought of you often, especially when the night is long and the sea is calm. I have imagined you as a child, running along the shore, your laughter echoing through the cliffs. But time has passed, and you have become a shadow of that girl, lost in the world beyond these islands.

I have always believed in the light, Eliza. Not just the light of the beacon, but the light of love, of duty, of hope. I have given my life to this lighthouse, to the safety of those who pass by, to the promise that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken and words ungiven. Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and though the sea has carried many a ship, it has not carried the words I have longed to say. I hope this letter finds you well, though I know not if you will read it—or if you even care.

The wind howls now, a sound that echoes the storm that rages outside. The waves lash against the cliffs with a fury that mirrors the tempest within me. I have spent my life standing here, watching the horizon, guiding the lost, and keeping the light burning. But tonight, for the first time in my long years, I fear that the light may go out before dawn.

You were but a child when you left, and I told myself that time would mend what was broken. But time, my dear, does not mend—it erodes. I have come to understand that I was not the father you needed, and that I was not the man you deserved. I was a man who thought duty was enough, who believed that the sea and the light were all that mattered. I failed to see the heart behind your eyes, the sorrow in your silence, the longing in your voice when you called me "Dad."

I have no excuse, only regret. I have no grand gestures, no grand words. Just this

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart that aches more than I can bear. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The lighthouse stands tall, though I fear it may not stand much longer. Tonight, I believe, will be my last night on this earth, and I must speak to you before the darkness takes me.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice. You were once my greatest joy, my pride, my very soul. And now... now you are a ghost in my mind, a shadow that haunts me when the storm clouds gather and the light fades.

I know I have been cruel. I know I have been distant. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once loved. I have let the sea and the silence of this lonely tower dull my heart. But I wish, with all my soul, that I could have been better. I wish I had not let pride and fear keep me from you.

You were the light in my life, Eleanor. The light that guided me through the darkest nights, the one who made me believe that even in this cruel world, there was still goodness, still love. And now, I fear, I have let that light fade.

I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I fear I may never see you again. Yet, as the winds howl and the sea roars with fury, I find myself compelled to speak, to confess, to apologize.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man of reason, let pride and fear keep me from reaching out. I told myself that you were better off without me, that I could not be the father you needed. But I was wrong. I was wrong in every way. I was a fool, a coward, and a man who let duty eclipse love.

Tonight, the storm is upon us. The sea, once my companion, now threatens to swallow the lighthouse whole. I have seen the light flicker, and I fear it may not shine again. I have spent my life watching the horizon, guiding ships through the dark, but tonight, I am the one who may not make it through.

I have no regrets for the life I’ve lived, but I do regret the life I’ve denied you. You were my light, my hope, and I let it fade. I pray that in some distant corner of the world, you have found peace, found love, found a father who truly understands.

If you read this, know that I am not the man I once was. I have

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of regret and the quill of a man who has long since lost the will to be anything but a keeper of the light—and of the silence that follows.

Five years have passed since you last set foot in this tower, and five years have not brought a single word from you. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, but the light has grown dimmer with each passing season. It is not the lantern that dims, but the heart that once knew how to shine.

The sea has been restless of late, and the wind carries with it the sound of your name, as though the waves themselves remember what was once ours. I have watched the storm gather on the horizon—dark, monstrous, and full of wrath. The sea is rising, and the sky is weeping as if the heavens themselves mourn. I have no doubt that this night will claim more than the waves.

I have not written to you before, for I feared that my words would be as empty as the silence that has settled between us. But tonight, as the tempest howls and the wind threatens to tear the roof from these ancient walls, I find myself longing to speak. Not to justify, not to beg, but to tell you what I have never dared: that I have not forgotten you. That I have not stopped looking for you. That I have not stopped loving you.

You were my light, El

My Dearest Clara,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars and the sky is a bruised, ashen hue, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, and if I do, I am not certain if I shall have the strength to write this. But I must—because I owe it to you.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and five years have done little to soften the ache of your absence. I have spent those years in this lonely tower, watching the waves and the stars, and in the silence, I have come to understand that I have been a failure as a father. I should have been there when you were a child, when you needed me most. I should have stayed when you left, instead of chasing after the world and forgetting the one who stayed behind.

I know you believe I abandoned you. And perhaps I did. I did not see the sorrow in your eyes when you left, nor did I hear the quiet plea in your voice. I was too busy with the light, too busy with duty, too blind to the truth that you were not a burden, but a part of me I had long since forgotten.

I have not stopped thinking of you, Clara. Every night, I sit by the window, watching the sea, and I imagine you standing there

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow. The wind howls like a beast from the sea, and the sea itself seems to rage in fury, as if it knows the weight of my silence. Tonight, I fear, may be my last. And yet, I must speak, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written a single word to you in all that time.

I have watched the storms come and go from the tower, but none have ever filled me with such dread as this one. The sky is black, the waves crash with a violence I have never seen, and the lighthouse light flickers like a dying soul. I have no doubt that I shall be gone by morning, and I pray that the sea will take me gently, as I once prayed for you.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who did not understand the weight of a father's heart, nor the burden of a man who must stand alone on a rock in the storm, watching the world pass by. I told you I would always be there, but I was not. I was too busy with the lamp, the fog, the endless nights, the endless work. I thought I could keep you safe from the world, and in doing so, I kept you from me.

You were not unkind, nor was I unkind. We were both lost in our own

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that feels as though it might shatter beneath the weight of words I have long kept locked away. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very cliffs that have cradled me these many years. Tonight, the storm comes with a vengeance, and I am not certain I shall see dawn.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, silent years. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and yet it lingers in my thoughts like the scent of the sea on a summer evening. I have not written to you, not even once, and I know the silence has been as cruel to you as it has been to me.

You were but a child when you left, and I was a man who had long since lost the will to be anything but a keeper of the light. I told myself that the sea was more forgiving than I was, and that the lighthouse would always be there, waiting, watching. But I have come to see that it was not the sea I feared, nor the loneliness, but the thought of you—of the life you might have had, the love you might have known, the future you might have built.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I have not forgotten the way your eyes filled with tears and your voice

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of the lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, as if it too knows the weight of the words I must now speak. Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time. Yet tonight—tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the waves grow wilder than ever—something stirs within me, a longing I cannot ignore.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. The sea has been my life, my work, my burden. I built this lighthouse with my own hands, and I have stood here night after night, watching the horizon, guiding the lost, and keeping the light burning. But I have never once thought of you. Not until now.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have your own life, your own path, and I have no right to intrude upon it. But I must say this: I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the day you left, the way you stood at the door, your eyes filled with sorrow and anger, and your voice trembling with what I could not hear. I have not forgotten the way you turned away, and I have not forgotten the silence that followed.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Bright, strong, and full of purpose. I watched you grow

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since grown cold, yet tonight, as the storm howls like a beast from the depths of hell, I find myself compelled to speak. The sea roars with a fury that shakes the very stones of this lonely tower, and I wonder if I shall see dawn. If I do, I shall not be the same man who once held you close in my arms. But if I do not, then perhaps this letter will be the last words you ever hear from your father.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of nights spent staring into the abyss of the sea, wondering if you had turned your back on me for good. I know the truth now, though it has taken me a long time to see it: you did not leave me out of spite or cruelty. You left because you could not bear the weight of what I had become. You left because you were tired of watching me wither like a dying flame, consumed by duty, by the sea, by the ghosts of those I failed.

I have not spoken of your mother, not since the day the tide took her from me. I buried her with my own hands, and since then, I have kept the lantern burning, not for her, but for myself. The light is a promise, Eleanor—a promise that I will not let the darkness claim this place, that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the dim glow of the lamp, its light flickering like the uncertain future I now face. The sea howls outside, a sound that has been my companion for over thirty years. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I’ve seen. The waves rise like mountains, and the wind howls with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I fear I may not see another dawn.

But I write this not with the hope of being heard, for I know you’ve long since turned your back on me. Yet still, I must try. For though the years have passed like sand through my fingers, I have never stopped thinking of you.

You were once my pride, my joy, my light in the darkness. I remember the day you left—your eyes cold, your voice sharp, and your heart closed to me. I tried to understand, to forgive, to forget. But I could not. You were the only child I ever had, and I was not prepared to lose you.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. That silence has grown heavy, like the weight of the sea on these rocks. I have watched the world change, and I have watched you change too. You no longer call me *Father*. You call me *nothing*. And I have let it go.

But tonight is different. Tonight, I fear I may not live to see the morning. The storm is coming, and I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same wretched state as I—dreading the future, haunted by the past, and more alone than I ever thought possible. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the weight of your absence still sits heavy upon my soul, like the fog that lingers in the early hours before dawn.

I write to you now not from the comfort of my cottage, but from the lighthouse itself, where I have spent the last three nights preparing for the storm that is upon us. The sea is restless, the winds grow louder, and the sky is bruised with the promise of ruin. I have lit the beacon, as I always do, and though it burns with a steady light, it feels like nothing more than a feeble attempt to ward off the darkness that has crept into my heart.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I do not know if you remember the last time we saw each other, or if you still remember who I am. I have tried to write to you, many times, but the letters were returned unopened, or perhaps they were lost at sea, like the boats that sometimes drift too close to the rocks and are never seen again. I suppose it is no wonder. You left, and I have not been worthy of your forgiveness—or your love.

I have always been a man of duty

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same old state—cold, unfeeling, and far from the heart I once knew. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the world has turned, I have not. I still stand here, night after night, in this lonely tower, watching the sea, listening to the wind, and wondering if I will ever see your face again.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. I have seen the sky darken with a fury that speaks of something unnatural. The sea, which has always been my companion, roars like a beast in pain. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have placed the last of the oil in its place. I am not afraid of the storm, not truly. I have faced worse than this in my years. But I am afraid for you, and for the life you’ve chosen.

I have not written to you in so long because I did not know what to say. Words, like the sea, can be both a comfort and a curse. I tried to reach you, but you turned your back, and I did not know how to follow. I thought, perhaps, that time would mend what was broken. But time, my dear, is not a healer—it is a thief.

You were my light, Eleanor. You were the reason I kept the lamp burning, even when the world

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw your face, and yet, the weight of that absence feels as though it has only just begun. I have kept your name in my mind, in my prayers, and in the flickering glow of the lighthouse lamp that watches over these lonely shores. But I fear the storm that brews on the horizon may take more than just the light—it may take me, and with me, the chance to speak to you once more.

You were but a child when you left, and I did not understand the fire that burned in your soul. You spoke of a life beyond this rock, beyond the sea, beyond the quiet rhythm of the waves and the salt in the air. I told you it was not for you. That this place, this duty, this life of solitude, was not the path you were meant to walk. And you left, as you always did—without a word, without a look, without a promise.

But I have never forgotten the way you stood at the door that last night, your eyes wide with a kind of sorrow I could not name. You looked at me not as a father, but as a stranger. And I did not know how to reach you. I did not know how to say the words that might have mended what had been broken. I only knew that the sea was

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since grown heavy with the weight of silence. Five years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and yet it feels as though the years have merely stretched the distance between us, like the endless miles of sea that lie beyond these cliffs.

You were a child when you left, and I, a man who believed that duty was more important than the love of a daughter. I told myself that the lighthouse was my family, that the light I kept burning was a promise to those who sailed the dark waters. But now, as I stand upon the narrow platform, the wind howling like a beast in the night, I am beginning to wonder if I was ever truly alone.

The storm is coming. I have seen the sky turn the color of iron, and the sea, once so calm and kind, now roars like a beast that has been awakened. The lantern is ready, the oil is primed, and the great bell hangs in its place, waiting for the call. But I do not know if I shall be here to see the dawn.

You were always so clever, Eleanor. I remember the way you would sit by the fire, your eyes alight with questions, your fingers tracing the maps I kept. You were the first to understand that the sea is not just water and wind, but a living thing, a force that must be respected. I was proud of

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I write to you from the lighthouse, where the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very sky. The night is dark, and the storm is coming—great and terrible. I have not seen the stars in days, and I fear I may not see them again.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last spoke to you. Time has been a cruel companion, and I have grown weary of its silence. I have tried to keep the lighthouse lit, to keep the beacon shining for those who sail the dark waters, but I have not been able to keep the light of my soul from fading. You were the only thing that ever made me feel truly whole, and now I am a man who has lost his way.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even care. I do not know if you still remember me, or if the years have made you forget the man who once held you in his arms and whispered promises of a better life. But I must write, for I have no one else to speak to. The sea has taken many, and the storm tonight may take me as well.

I have not written to you because I feared what I might say. I have not written because I feared what you might think of me. But now

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and with a heart that feels as though it may crack beneath the weight of what I must say. Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I fear the storm that now gathers on the horizon may not be the only thing to claim me.

You were never meant to leave, Eliza. Not like that. I know you must have thought I was cruel, that I turned my back on you when you needed me most. But I did not turn my back. I only turned my face, for I could not bear the sight of your sorrow, nor the truth that I had failed you in every way that mattered.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who had long since forgotten how to be a father. I told myself that time would mend what had been broken, that distance would make the pain fade. But time has only made it worse, and distance has only left me with silence.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for twenty years now, and I have watched the sea with more than just my eyes. I have watched it take lives, and I have watched it take hope. And I have watched it take me, piece by piece, with each passing year.

Tonight, the storm comes. It is not the kind that merely shakes the stones of this cliff—it is the kind that will shake the very bones of the

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lamps have been lit, as they must be, for the sailors who depend upon them, though I know they may not see them in the chaos of this storm. I have been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and I have seen many storms, but never one like this. It is as if the heavens themselves are in turmoil, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not written to you in five years, and I have not spoken to you in even longer. I know you have no reason to believe this letter will reach you, and I do not expect you to answer. But I must say these things, for I have spent too long silent, and the silence has grown heavy upon my soul.

You were a child when I last saw you, and I have spent the years since in the solitude of this tower, watching the sea and the sky, and remembering. I have not forgotten the day you left. I have not forgotten the tears, the anger, the pain. I do not blame you for what you did, nor do I blame myself. We were both young, and we were both lost in our own ways. You chose your path, and I chose mine. And though I have not walked it with you, I have walked

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm clouds that gather on the horizon. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this: I have never stopped loving you.

I have watched the sea for years, and I have seen many storms. I have seen the light flicker in the dark, and I have seen the darkness claim the light. But tonight is different. The sky is black, the waves are monstrous, and I feel the weight of the world pressing upon my old bones. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, but never have I felt so small.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with a heart full of fire and a mind full of dreams. You thought the world was too small for you, and you ran from it. I did not understand at first, but I have come to see that you were not running from me. You were running from the world I had built for you—a world of duty, of silence, of duty. You wanted more. You wanted to be more.

I have missed you, my child. I have missed your laughter, your questions, your stubbornness. I have missed the way you would sit

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind screams through the cracks in the lighthouse walls as though it seeks to tear it down, as though it seeks to tear *me* down. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a lifetime.

I have always been a man of routine, of duty, of quiet resolve. The lighthouse is my life, my purpose, my anchor in a world that has long since drifted from me. But tonight, as I stand before the beacon, I feel it waver—not in the light, but in my soul. The storm is not just a storm, Eleanor. It is a reckoning. A reminder that I have failed you, just as I have failed so many others.

I know you do not want to hear from me. You have every right not to. After all, I left you when you were still a child, and I have not returned since. I told myself I was doing what was right, that I was protecting you from the world’s cruelty. But I was wrong. I was selfish. I chose the sea over you, the silence over the truth, and now I am left with nothing but regret.

I have not forgotten the days we spent by the shore, the way

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Time, like the sea, has a way of swallowing things whole, and I have not heard from you in five long years. I have written to you, I have sent letters, but they have returned unopened, or worse, they have been lost in the storm, like so many of the ships that pass by our lighthouse.

You were but a child when you left, and I thought I could keep you safe. But you were never meant to be here, in this place where the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea is never still. You were meant for the world beyond, for society, for life. And so you left, as you always have—without a word, without a farewell.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I should now. But tonight—a terrible storm is coming. The sky is black as ink, and the waves are rising like the hands of the damned. I have seen worse storms, but this one… it feels different. It feels like the end. And I am not sure I will survive it.

I have spent my life watching the sea, guiding the lost, and keeping the light burning. But I have never been able to guide you, nor keep you from the path you chose. I have tried, Eleanor. I have tried to be the father

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I, for I have not been well of late. The sea has been restless, as it often is in the dead of winter, and the wind howls through the cliffs like a wounded beast. I sit here by the light, the great lamp burning with a steady glow, as if it knows what is coming. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the morning.

You were never one for letters, my dear. You always said they were too slow, too uncertain, and that words could never carry the weight of a father’s heart. But tonight, as I watch the waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have never seen, I feel the need to speak. To say what I have never said before.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since I last held you in my arms, since I last heard your laughter echo through this old lighthouse. I have kept the light burning for you, Eleanor. I have watched the dawn break over the sea, and I have waited for you to come back. But you did not. And I did not write.

I know you blame me. I know you think I failed you. But I did not fail you, Eleanor. I tried. I tried to be the father you needed. I tried to be the man you deserved. And yet, I was not enough. And I know now that I

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the stone that guards the lighthouse. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not seen the light of day in days, and the storm is upon us with a fury that makes me fear for my life—*and for yours*.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the night when the world is still and the sea is calm. I have not written, not even when the loneliness gnawed at my soul like the rats that infest the lower chambers. You were my light, my only light, and now you are gone—like the beacon that once guided your way.

You told me I was a fool, that I had chosen duty over family, that I had let the sea take everything from me. And I said nothing. I could not. I was too proud, too ashamed, too afraid to explain. But now, as I stand here, the wind tearing at my coat and the waves crashing against the cliffs, I wish I had. I wish I had fought for you, for us.

**April 12th, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the glass of the lighthouse. The sea roars, and the sky is black as pitch, save for the occasional flash of lightning that reveals the jagged teeth of the cliffs. I have not slept in days, and now, with the storm upon us, I fear I may not live to see dawn.

But I must write, for if I do not, I shall regret it for the rest of my days. You are my only child, my heart's truest sorrow and my greatest regret. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though I have not forgotten you, I have grown weary of the silence between us.

But I write it anyway, because I have nothing left to lose, and everything to say.

You left me, Eliza. Not in anger, but in silence. You told me you could not bear to live in the same house, the same life, the same grief. I did not understand at first. I thought perhaps it was the sea, or the loneliness, or the weight of the world pressing down on you. But time has taught me that it was you, and only you, who could not bear to stay.

I have kept the lighthouse, not for the glory, nor for the wages, but because it is all I have left. I have kept the light burning, not for the ships, but for the hope that one day, you might return. I have kept the light burning because I cannot bear to let it go.

The light that guided me through the darkest nights of my years. And now, I wonder if I have become a shadow of the man you once knew.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I remember the day you left, the way your eyes brimmed with tears and your voice cracked as you said, *“You don’t understand.”* I thought I did. I thought I had done what was right.

You were angry. I was wrong. But I never meant to hurt you. I thought I was protecting you from the world, from the cruel truths I had seen. I thought I was shielding you from the sin that clings to my soul. But I was wrong, and I know now that I was only shrouding you in shadows.
